milk_based.name: Caffè Latte
milk_based.description: The classic espresso and steamed milk combination. Silky, smooth, and endlessly customizable.
steps: Pull two shots of espresso into a 280ml cup.
Steam milk to 65°C with a tight, silky micro-foam texture.
Pour steamed milk from low height, then lift to create a white dot on top.
Optionally draw a simple latte art pattern.
milk_based.tip: The key to a great latte is properly textured milk — no bubbles, just smooth glossy foam.

milk_based.name: Cappuccino
milk_based.description: Equal parts espresso, steamed milk, and thick foam. Rich, bold, and texturally complex.
steps: Pull two espresso shots into a 180ml ceramic cup.
Steam milk to create thick, stiff foam (not silky like a latte).
Pour a small amount of steamed milk, then spoon thick foam generously on top.
Optionally dust with cinnamon or cocoa powder.
milk_based.tip: Traditional Italian cappuccino is always 180ml total — avoid oversized versions.

milk_based.name: Cortado
milk_based.description: Espresso 'cut' with a small amount of warm milk to reduce acidity. Bold but balanced.
steps: Pull two espresso shots into a glass tumbler (Gibraltar glass ideal).
Warm milk to 60°C — minimal foam, almost flat.
Pour milk directly over espresso in a 1:1 ratio.
Serve immediately without stirring.
milk_based.tip: The milk is not steamed with foam — it's simply warmed to take the edge off the espresso.

milk_based.name: Flat White
milk_based.description: Smaller and stronger than a latte, with a higher espresso-to-milk ratio and velvety micro-foam.
steps: Pull a double ristretto (shorter, sweeter extraction) into a 160ml cup.
Steam milk to 62°C with very fine, velvety micro-foam — thinner than a latte.
Pour milk with a free-pour latte art technique.
The result should be mostly milk with a thin layer of foam on top.
milk_based.tip: Use ristretto (not espresso) for a sweeter, less bitter base that shines through the milk.

milk_based.name: Espresso Macchiato
milk_based.description: Espresso 'stained' with a dollop of foam. The purist's compromise between straight espresso and milk drinks.
steps: Pull a double espresso into a small demitasse cup.
Steam a small amount of milk to create a spoonful of stiff foam.
Spoon or pour a small amount of foam onto the espresso crema.
Serve immediately.
milk_based.tip: Macchiato means 'stained' in Italian — the milk should only lightly mark the espresso, not overwhelm it.

cold.name: Espresso Tonic
cold.description: A refreshing summer drink — the bitterness of espresso floats beautifully on sparkling tonic. Surprisingly complex.
steps: Fill a tall glass with ice cubes.
Pour cold tonic water over the ice (pour down the side to preserve carbonation).
Slowly pour hot espresso shots over the back of a spoon to create a layered effect.
Garnish with a thin lemon slice. Do not stir — enjoy the layers.
cold.tip: Use a high-quality, less-sweet tonic (Fever-Tree or similar). The bitterness balance is everything.

cold.name: Cold Brew
cold.description: Slow cold extraction over 12–24 hours creates an ultra-smooth, low-acid coffee concentrate.
steps: Combine coarse ground coffee and cold water in a large jar. Stir briefly.
Cover and refrigerate for 12–24 hours.
Strain through a fine mesh strainer lined with a coffee filter.
Serve over ice. Dilute 1:1 with water or milk for a regular strength cup.
cold.tip: Cold brew keeps in the fridge for up to 2 weeks. Make a big batch!

cold.name: Iced Latte
cold.description: Classic espresso over ice and cold milk. Simple, refreshing, and endlessly popular.
steps: Pull two shots of espresso and allow to cool slightly (or brew directly over ice).
Fill a 350ml glass with ice cubes.
Pour cold milk over the ice.
Add espresso shots over the milk. Stir and serve.
cold.tip: Brewing espresso directly over ice (Japanese iced coffee method) preserves aromatic volatiles better than cooling separately.

cold.name: Nitro Cold Brew
cold.description: Cold brew infused with nitrogen gas for a creamy, Guinness-like cascade and naturally sweet taste.
ingredients: nitrogen gas (ISI whipper or keg system)
steps: Prepare cold brew concentrate (1:4 ratio, 24 hour steep).
Charge cold brew in a whipped cream dispenser with 1–2 nitrogen cartridges.
Refrigerate charged dispenser for at least 2 hours.
Pour into a glass at an angle and watch the cascade form. Serve immediately.
cold.tip: A cream whipper with N₂O cartridges works as a home nitro system — don't shake before dispensing.

cold.name: Vietnamese Iced Coffee
cold.description: Strong Robusta drip coffee with sweetened condensed milk over ice. Sweet, rich, and intensely caffeinated.
steps: Place condensed milk at the bottom of a glass.
Brew coffee through a Phin filter (Vietnamese drip filter) directly over the condensed milk.
Allow coffee to brew slowly for 3–5 minutes.
Fill a separate glass with ice. Pour the coffee-milk mixture over the ice and stir.
cold.tip: Traditional Vietnamese coffee uses Robusta beans — stronger and more bitter than Arabica, which is what makes this drink work with condensed milk.

experimental.name: Salted Caramel Misto
experimental.description: A creative take on the café misto — drip coffee meets caramel-salted steamed milk for a dessert-like experience.
steps: Brew strong drip coffee and keep warm.
Warm caramel sauce in the milk before steaming — stir to combine.
Steam caramel-milk mixture to 65°C.
Combine equal parts coffee and steamed caramel milk in a large cup. Top with a pinch of flaky sea salt.
experimental.tip: Fleur de sel or Maldon sea salt provides the best contrast against the sweet caramel.

experimental.name: Dalgona Coffee
experimental.description: Whipped instant coffee foam over cold milk. The viral coffee trend that actually tastes incredible.
steps: Combine instant coffee, sugar, and hot water in a bowl.
Whip vigorously by hand or with an electric mixer for 3–5 minutes until stiff, caramel-colored foam forms.
Fill a glass with ice and pour cold milk.
Spoon whipped coffee foam on top. Stir before drinking.
experimental.tip: The sugar is not optional — it's what stabilizes the foam. Reduce sweetness by using half sugar, half erythritol.

experimental.name: Coffee Negroni
experimental.description: The classic Negroni riff with cold brew replacing gin. Bold, bitter, complex, and surprisingly sophisticated.
steps: Combine cold brew concentrate, sweet vermouth, and Campari in a mixing glass with ice.
Stir 20–30 times until well chilled and slightly diluted.
Strain into a rocks glass over one large ice cube.
Express orange peel over the drink and use as garnish.
experimental.tip: This is non-alcoholic as written but use the same ratios with gin added for a full Coffee Gin Negroni.

experimental.name: Golden Coffee Latte
experimental.description: Espresso meets golden milk — turmeric, ginger, and black pepper meet coffee for an anti-inflammatory, warming drink.
steps: Warm oat milk in a small saucepan over medium heat.
Whisk in turmeric, ginger, black pepper, and honey until smooth.
Pull a single espresso shot into a large mug.
Froth the golden milk using a handheld frother or steam wand.
Pour golden milk over espresso. Top with a dusting of turmeric.
experimental.tip: Black pepper is essential — it activates curcumin (turmeric's active compound) and improves absorption.
